title.emphasis: Part III: Liability Under California's Unruh Civil Rights Act – Denial of Service as Arbitrary Discrimination

section.para: The pattern of arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement by payment processors exposes them to significant legal liability under California's Unruh Civil Rights Act. As the state's primary public accommodations law, the Unruh Act provides a powerful legal framework for challenging the denial of services by business establishments when that denial is based on arbitrary classifications or targets individuals based on protected characteristics.

title.emphasis: 3.1 Payment Processors as "Business Establishments" Under California Law

section.para: The Unruh Act applies broadly to "all business establishments of every kind whatsoever".40 California courts have consistently interpreted this language in the "broadest sense reasonably possible," explicitly extending its reach beyond traditional brick-and-mortar locations to include online businesses, financial institutions, and other digital service providers.42 As providers of essential commercial services to the public, payment processors like PayPal and Stripe squarely fit within this expansive definition and are therefore subject to the Act's anti-discrimination mandates.

title.emphasis: 3.2 The Provision of Financial Services as "Accommodations, Advantages, Facilities, Privileges or Services"

section.para: The Unruh Act guarantees all persons "full and equal accommodations, advantages, facilities, privileges or services" from covered businesses.45 In the context of the modern digital economy, the ability to accept online payments is not merely an "advantage" but a fundamental "privilege" and "service" necessary for any business to operate and compete. Denying a merchant access to a payment processing account is tantamount to barring them from the marketplace, making this service a core privilege protected by the Act.

title.emphasis: 3.3 Arbitrary Discrimination Based on Protected Characteristics

section.para: The central claim under the Unruh Act is that the de-platforming of creators based on the content of their legally protected expression constitutes arbitrary discrimination. The Act explicitly prohibits discrimination based on "sex" and "sexual orientation".40 A policy that results in the blanket prohibition of legal, sex-positive content or disproportionately targets LGBTQ+-themed expression can be framed as a form of discrimination based on these protected characteristics.

Crucially, a business can only defend against an Unruh Act claim if it can demonstrate a "legitimate, nondiscriminatory business reason" for its actions.42 Here, the processors' likely defense—that their policies are necessary for managing financial and reputational risk—is directly undermined by the evidence of inconsistent application. The differential treatment of online gambling (permitted with oversight) and legal adult content (often banned outright) demonstrates that the policy is not a neutral application of a risk-based standard. This inconsistency allows a plaintiff to argue that the stated business reason is pretextual. When the pretextual justification is stripped away, the action is revealed as arbitrary, content-based discrimination, which is prohibited by the Unruh Act when it impacts individuals based on their association with a protected class.
